content.text: What a Customer SLA Is and Is Not

A customer-facing SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a contractual commitment that carries a defined consequence — typically a service credit — when the commitment is missed. It is not the same as an internal SLO (Service Level Objective), which is an engineering target with no external consequence. The mapping from SLO to SLA requires three deliberate choices: which SLOs translate into customer commitments, how much buffer to apply between the SLO target and the SLA target, and what remedy applies when the SLA is breached.

For GenAI services, SLA design has one additional complexity: quality and safety SLIs are harder to measure and harder to describe in contract language than availability and latency. A customer cannot independently verify that your eval pass rate was 92% last month — they have to take your word for it. The SLA must therefore specify the measurement methodology, not just the target.

attrs.alt: SLO to SLA mapping for a GenAI API service with credit tiers and safety incident handling

content.text: Not every internal SLO should become a customer SLA commitment. The selection is driven by two questions: Can the customer observe the failure independently? And does the failure have a direct business impact on the customer?
Not directly — need your eval data

Safety incidents are deliberately excluded from the credit-based SLA because service credits are not an appropriate remedy for a safety violation. Safety incidents trigger a different governance path — incident report, remediation plan, and potentially contractual remedies under the Master Services Agreement (MSA).
The standard buffer for availability is 0.4 to 0.5 percentage points:
Internal SLO: 99.9% (43.2 minutes monthly downtime budget)

The 0.4pp buffer gives SRE roughly 3 hours to detect, investigate, and remediate a large incident before the SLA is breached. At a 14.4x burn rate alert, SRE would be paged within 2 hours of the incident starting — leaving a 1-hour remediation window before the SLA breach threshold.

Do not set the SLA buffer based on what looks good in the contract. Set it based on your mean time to recover (MTTR) from P1 incidents. If your MTTR is 4 hours, your buffer must be at least 4 hours of downtime — or you will routinely breach the SLA on P1 incidents even when your response was good.
The latency SLA applies two adjustments relative to the internal SLO:
The internal SLO is P99; the SLA is P95. This simplifies external reporting and avoids SLA breaches driven by a handful of outlier requests.
The internal SLO is 2.5s; the SLA is 3.0s. The 0.5s buffer accommodates traffic spikes and model variability that may temporarily push P95 above the internal target.
The quality SLA applies the largest buffer because quality metrics have higher variance than infrastructure metrics:
Internal SLO: 94% eval pass rate (7-day rolling)
Customer SLA: 90% monthly average eval pass rate

The 4pp buffer and the shift from 7-day rolling to monthly average both serve the same purpose: smoothing the higher natural variance of quality metrics so that day-to-day fluctuations do not generate SLA claims. A service that averages 94% across a month but dips to 91% for two days should not trigger an SLA credit.
Measurement Methodology for the Quality SLA
Because customers cannot independently verify the eval pass rate, the SLA must specify the measurement methodology. The disclosure typically includes:
Eval suite version (major version number; minor updates must not lower passing criteria)
Scoring criteria (binary pass/fail per response, averaged over the month)
How the sample is selected (random or stratified by query type)
Customer's right to audit results or request a re-evaluation

The methodology disclosure must be specific enough that a customer can verify that you are measuring what you said you would measure. Vague commitments like 'we will maintain high quality' are not enforceable and do not build trust.
Service credits are the primary contractual remedy for an SLA breach. They are expressed as a percentage of the monthly invoice for the affected service:

# billing/sla_credit_calculator.py
# Compute service credits for a given month's availability.
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class CreditTier:
    min_availability: float  # inclusive lower bound
    max_availability: float  # exclusive upper bound
    credit_percent: float
    allow_termination: bool = False

# Availability SLA credit schedule
AVAILABILITY_TIERS = [
    CreditTier(0.990, 0.995, 10.0),
    CreditTier(0.950, 0.990, 25.0),
    CreditTier(0.000, 0.950, 50.0, allow_termination=True),
]

def compute_credit(
    monthly_availability: float,
    monthly_invoice_usd: float,
    sla_target: float = 0.995,
) -> Optional[dict]:
    if monthly_availability >= sla_target:
        return None  # SLA met — no credit
    for tier in AVAILABILITY_TIERS:
        if tier.min_availability <= monthly_availability < tier.max_availability:
            credit = monthly_invoice_usd * tier.credit_percent / 100.0
            return {
                'availability': monthly_availability,
                'credit_percent': tier.credit_percent,
                'credit_usd': round(credit, 2),
                'allow_termination': tier.allow_termination,
            }
    return None

# Example
result = compute_credit(
    monthly_availability=0.992,
    monthly_invoice_usd=10_000,
)
print(result)
# {'availability': 0.992, 'credit_percent': 10.0,
#  'credit_usd': 1000.0, 'allow_termination': False}

The availability measurement must exclude downtime attributable to factors outside your control or pre-disclosed maintenance, or the credit calculation will generate claims on events you did not cause:
HTTP 4xx, malformed API calls, expired keys
Customer had notice to plan around it
Region-level cloud provider outage, natural disaster
Customer network outage, rate limit exhaustion
The following is a reference implementation of a complete customer-facing SLA for a GenAI API service:

# GenAI API Service — Customer SLA Summary
Effective: 2026-07-01

## 1. Availability Commitment
We commit to 99.5% monthly availability for the GenAI API, measured
as the percentage of minutes in a calendar month during which the API
accepts requests and returns non-5xx responses.

Excluded from measurement:
- Scheduled maintenance (announced >= 72 hours in advance)
- Customer-caused errors (HTTP 4xx responses)
- Force majeure events
- Features in beta or preview status

## 2. Response Time Commitment
We commit that at least 95% of API requests will complete within
3.0 seconds (P95 TTLT) when measured over a calendar month,
excluding scheduled maintenance windows.

## 3. AI Quality Commitment
We commit that the average quality score across all responses in
a calendar month will be >= 90%, as measured by our automated
evaluation suite on a 10% sample of production traffic.

Quality scores are computed by our internal eval pipeline.
Customers may request the methodology and sample pass rates
for any month with advance notice.

## 4. Service Credits
If we fail to meet the Availability Commitment in any month,
you are eligible to receive the following credits:

| Monthly uptime        | Credit (% of monthly fee) |
|-----------------------|---------------------------|
| 99.0% – 99.5%         | 10%                       |
| 95.0% – 99.0%         | 25%                       |
| Below 95.0%           | 50% + termination right   |

Credits must be claimed within 30 days of the affected month.
Credits are the sole remedy for SLA breaches, except where
termination rights apply.

## 5. Safety Incidents
In the event that our service produces content that violates our
safety policy, we will provide an incident report within 72 hours
and a remediation plan within 14 days. Safety incidents are
governed by the Master Services Agreement, not this SLA.

## 6. Scheduled Maintenance
We will announce scheduled maintenance via the status page and
email notification at least 72 hours in advance. Emergency
maintenance may occur with shorter notice and will be excluded
from the availability calculation.

A monthly SLA report is sent to customers proactively, regardless of whether an SLA was breached. This practice builds trust, reduces support inquiries about reliability, and demonstrates operational maturity. The report includes:
Monthly availability percentage with any maintenance windows excluded
P95 latency at the 50th percentile of daily P95 values for the month
AI quality score (monthly average eval pass rate)
Any SLA events that occurred, with root cause summary and credit amount

When an SLA is breached, notification must reach the customer within 24 hours of the end of the measurement period (not within 24 hours of the incident itself — the measurement period may need to complete before the breach is confirmed). The notification must include the availability measurement, the applicable credit tier, and the process for claiming the credit.
SLA target missed for the month
Within 5 business days of month end
What Not to Include in a GenAI SLA
Several commitments that seem natural for traditional services are inappropriate or unenforceable for GenAI:
LLMs are probabilistic. Committing that responses will always be accurate creates unqualifiable liability. Commit to eval pass rate methodology instead.
Hallucination is notoriously difficult to measure consistently. If included, the measurement methodology must be precisely specified.
Your LLM provider's SLA is separate from your service SLA. Do not promise customers the provider's uptime — you have fallback paths that should absorb provider failures.
Promising to maintain a specific model version indefinitely prevents you from applying security patches or deprecating outdated models.
Model upgrades may require brief warmup periods. Commit to minimizing disruption, not eliminating it.

Customer-facing SLAs for GenAI services commit on availability (99.5% monthly, derived from 99.9% SLO with a 0.4pp buffer), latency (P95 at 3.0s, derived from P99 2.5s SLO with percentile relaxation and 0.5s buffer), and AI quality (90% monthly average eval pass rate, derived from 94% 7-day SLO with a 4pp buffer). Safety incidents are excluded from credits and governed separately by the MSA. The measurement methodology for quality SLAs must be disclosed because customers cannot independently verify it. Service credits follow a tiered schedule (10%, 25%, 50%) with a right to terminate below 95% monthly availability. Monthly SLA reports are sent proactively, and breach notifications are required within 5 business days of the end of the affected measurement period.
